entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Drawing on material culture, primary sources, and wider overviews, this course delves into the time period from 100 BC to 350 AD.

This topic is included in the following course(s): History: Grades 10-12 (U.S.), History: Grade 10 (Canada), History: Grades 11-12 (Canada)

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Inquisitive 9th graders could enjoy this course, but the main texts have an advanced reading level, so students would likely want to move at a slower pace.

rows.label: Ancient History: Grades 10-12
rows.scheduleInfo:
Ancient History: Grades 10-12
1 time/week 30 min
1 time/week 40 min
rows.books: Early Christian Writings: The Apostolic Fathers edited||Ancient Empires: From Mesopotamia to the Rise of Islam||Christians Reading Classics

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

books.title: Early Christian Writings: The Apostolic Fathers edited
Ancient Empires: From Mesopotamia to the Rise of Islam

blocks.text:
• Take a second to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

• If the previous passage is difficult to remember, try looking back at any chapter/section headings or illustrations or reading the first paragraph to jog the memory.

• Read the selection assigned OR read for the number of minutes allocated, leaving time for narration and mapwork. Use whichever guide (i.e., pages or minutes) works best for you. You want to read enough to be interesting, but not so much that you feel overwhelmed.

• If a book is challenging for you as a student, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently for a minute every now and then to make sure you are tracking. You might also want to read aloud to yourself. Or you might want to listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along with it. 

• If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! 

• Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your commonplace book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

• Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. 

• To keep yourself on track, you might want to set a timer, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

• After your narration, think about how what you read connects to other subjects, other books, and other things you've read, watched, or seen in the past or present. 

• Share any ideas that are particularly interesting to you with your fellow classmates, teachers, parents, friends, or others. You might even want to do a bit more reading or research on something on your own time in the afternoon or evenings.

These lessons are written directly to the student, but to assist them throughout the year, please read through this section and the lesson plans so you are familiar with the instructions and flow of the lessons. Accountability components are added to facilitate teacher/student discussion and growing independence. 

Those educating at home might pick one or two history lessons to read aloud with their students to keep up the habit of oral narration and foster conversation and relationship, and then simply give students these lesson plans and the rest of the books and check in with them throughout the term for conversation, support, and accountability. 

It is valuable for those in a classroom setting to also have a mix of lessons that students complete themselves silently and others read aloud and narrated together. Classroom settings lend themselves well to additional discussion and sharing at the end of lessons, after everyone has narrated either together or independently in writing.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Welcome to the Empire
⍞ Materials: Ancient Empires

➜ INTRO 
What comes to your mind when you hear the word "empire?" Historians disagree on many things including times and dates, translations, and terminology. One of the terms that is debated is the word "empire" and its meaning. As you read through the introduction to Ancient Empires, what definition do you agree with? Why do you agree with it? You're invited to answer these questions aloud or write them down.

➜ ANNOTATE
Ancient Empires is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return too. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Introduction: "What is an (Ancient) Empire?" p.1-9 
If you have already read this resource, just review the Introduction section assigned here.

➜ WRITE
Explain the IEMP model and it's importance in reading ancient history in 1-2 paragraphs.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ STUDENT TIP
Use sticky notes to mark/note significant people, events, artifacts, etc. while reading to help you remember to put them in your Book of Centuries.

lessons.title: 40m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
The Late Republic
⍞ Materials: Ancient Empires

➜ INTRO
Rome was a conquering nation. It had bested the Greeks and it had best many of it's neighbors including the Carthage, which was a sovereign state until it was razed to the ground in 146 B.C in the Third Punic War. But Rome didn't stop with Carthage. It expanded it's armies, towns and peoples and would come to be one of the largest empires to ever exist and leaving a legacy of greatness, blood and death.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.9 p.210-214 start at “the Late Republic” and stop at "Spartacus in 74-71 B.C."

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: From Enslavement to Rebel Gladiator: The Life of Spartacus

➜ WRITE
Summarize either the reading or the video. What did you learn and what were you already familiar with?
lessons.teacherNotes:
∞ Video Link: From Enslavement to Rebel Gladiator: The Life of Spartacus

• STUDENT TIP 
Be sure to add some people, events, and drawings of artifacts to your Book of Centuries. Mark potential items with a Post-it note as you read so you remember where to find something when you sit down to work in your book.

links.text: From Enslavement to Rebel Gladiator: The Life of Spartacus

lessons.body:
A New Political Order
⍞ Materials: Ancient Empires
 
➜ INTRO
Caesar's assassination does not save the republic as Brutus and Cassius believe it will. And the heir to Caesar's seat of power will become the very thing that ends Roman Republic.
 
➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.10 p.221-226 end at “of social power”

➜ MAPWORK
Examine the maps on p.223-224. What stands out to you about these maps?

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
How do Christians Read Classics?
⍞ Materials: Christians Reading Classics
 
➜ INTRO
Can Christians read Greco-Roman pagan literature for spiritual formation? This is the question that Nadya Williams asks in the introduction of her book. As you read through the introduction, come up with an answer to this question. 
 
➜ ANNOTATE
Christians Reading Classics is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return too. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.
 
➜ READ & NARRATE
Introduction: p.xi-xx
∞ Link: Interview with Nadya Williams 
 
➜ WRITE
Do you think that Christians can read Greco-Roman pagan literature for spiritual formation? Are writings such as Homer, Boethius, Virgil or other ancient pagan writers spiritually beneficial? Why or why not?
 
➜ PONDER
What in the Interview with Nadya Williams stands out to you?
lessons.teacherNotes:
∞ Link: Interview with Nadya Williams

★ STUDENT TIP
Remember to mark people and events for your Book of Centuries.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
Cicero, Caesar, and Political Rhetoric in the Roman Republic
⍞ Materials: Christians Reading Classics

➜ INTRO
The political sphere of the Roman Empire is a cut-throat business. So much so, that if one person was elected, their life might be immediately in danger as their opponent may plot an assassination for the winning man. Such is the case of Cicero. As you'll be able to see in the reading, these documents tell us so much more than just an assassination attempt.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.11 p.143-151 at “his family home”

Reading Cicero
⍞ Materials: Cicero's First Speech Against Catiline
 
➜ INTRO
After uncovering a plot by a politician and soldier named Catiline who was going use violence and arson to overthrow the government. This speech was delivered in front of the senate. While you read through the speech, make sure to take note of the things you find interesting and write down any questions you have. 
 
➜ READ & NARRATE
p.1-7

lessons.title: 40m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 72
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
The Aeneid, Early Christian Writings, Ancient Empires, and online resources

terms.content:
· In what ways does the Roman Republic remind you of your own country? In what ways is it different?

·  The Aeneid: Describe the character of Aeneas. What do you think are his defining traits? Give a few concrete examples of where you see them in the Aeneid. 

· Draw two pieces of art that you remember seeing this term. Briefly say what they are and describe them. What do you think they say about the cultures that they were a part of?

· Ancient Empires: What does the IEMP model use for an approach to studying Ancient History?

· Based on your readings in Ancient Empires this term, how do you think that the Roman Empire was able to hold together such a large group of people? How was the empire different from other empires you have studied, or from the Roman Republic that preceded it?

·  Choose one:
- In book six of the Aeneid, Vergil describes Aeneas’ journey into the underworld. How does he describe the underworld? What does it tell you about the Roman view of the afterlife and how is that description similar or different from your beliefs about life after death?
- Draw two pieces of art that you remember seeing this term. Briefly say what they are and describe them. What do you think they say about the cultures that they were a part of? 
- Early Christian Writings & "Martyrdom of Ignatius" online resource: Compare the martyrdom stories that you read of Polycarp and Ignatius of Antioch. What do they have in common? How are they different? What character traits do both men share?

·  Choose one: 
- The most common adjective that Vergil uses to describe Aeneas is “pious.” Is this the adjective you would have used to describe his character? From the books of the Aeneid you have read, tell what ways this piety is exemplified and why you think this was important to Vergil.
- Early Christian Writings: Based on the writings of the early church fathers that you have read this term, what were some of the primary concerns of the early church? What are the most common themes? Are the concerns of the early church the same as or different than the concerns of the Christian church today?
- Describe some of the accusations that the Romans made against the early Christians and how they are clarified or refuted by the apologist Justin Martyr.
- Ancient Empires: How did Diocletian and Constantine help to resolve the crises of the 3rd century? What role did Christianity play in both creating and resolving some of the problems in the late Roman Empire?
